questions.questionText: If the point of intersection of the lines $ \frac{x+1}{3} = \frac{y+a}{5} = \frac{z+b+1}{7} $ and $ \frac{x-2}{1} = \frac{y-b}{4} = \frac{z-2a}{7} $ lies on xy-plane, then the value of $a+b$ is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let the common point of intersection of the two lines be $P$.

Since the point lies on the $xy$-plane, its $z$-coordinate must be $0$.

We write both lines in parametric form.

For the first line,

$$ \frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+a}{5}=\frac{z+b+1}{7}=\lambda $$

So,

$$ x=3\lambda-1,\qquad y=5\lambda-a,\qquad z=7\lambda-b-1 $$

For the second line,

$$ \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-b}{4}=\frac{z-2a}{7}=\mu $$

So,

$$ x=\mu+2,\qquad y=4\mu+b,\qquad z=7\mu+2a $$

At the point of intersection, coordinates are equal.

So we get:

$$ 3\lambda-1=\mu+2 $$

$$ 5\lambda-a=4\mu+b $$

$$ 7\lambda-b-1=7\mu+2a $$

Also, since the point lies on the $xy$-plane,

$$ z=0 $$

Hence from both lines:

$$ 7\lambda-b-1=0 $$

and

$$ 7\mu+2a=0 $$

Now solve step by step.

From

$$ 3\lambda-1=\mu+2 $$

we get

$$ 3\lambda-\mu=3 \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad \mu=3\lambda-3 $$

From $z=0$ in the first line,

$$ b=7\lambda-1 $$

From $z=0$ in the second line,

$$ 2a=-7\mu \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad a=-\frac{7\mu}{2} $$

Now use

$$ 5\lambda-a=4\mu+b $$

Substitute $\mu=3\lambda-3$, $b=7\lambda-1$:

$$ 5\lambda-a=4(3\lambda-3)+(7\lambda-1) $$

$$ 5\lambda-a=12\lambda-12+7\lambda-1 $$

$$ 5\lambda-a=19\lambda-13 $$

$$ -a=14\lambda-13 $$

$$ a=13-14\lambda $$

Also,

$$ a=-\frac{7\mu}{2}=-\frac{7(3\lambda-3)}{2} $$

$$ a=\frac{-21\lambda+21}{2} $$

Equate the two values of $a$:

$$ 13-14\lambda=\frac{-21\lambda+21}{2} $$

Multiply by $2$:

$$ 26-28\lambda=-21\lambda+21 $$

$$ 5=7\lambda $$

$$ \lambda=\frac{5}{7} $$

Then

$$ \mu=3\lambda-3=3\cdot \frac{5}{7}-3=\frac{15}{7}-\frac{21}{7}=-\frac{6}{7} $$

Now find $a$ and $b$.

$$ b=7\lambda-1=5-1=4 $$

$$ a=-\frac{7\mu}{2}=-\frac{7}{2}\left(-\frac{6}{7}\right)=3 $$

Therefore,

$$ a+b=3+4=7 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{7} $$

Option C

questions.questionText: Let a line L passing through the point (1, 1, 1) be perpendicular to both the vectors $2\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + \hat{k}$ and $\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}$. If $P(a, b, c)$ is the foot of perpendicular from the origin on the line L, then the value of $34(a + b + c)$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Direction vector of line is

$$ =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} $$

So equation of line is

$$ \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z-1}{2}=\lambda \text { let } $$

let ( $2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda+1$ ) be the foot of perpendicular from $(0,0,0)$ upon given line

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \mathrm{j}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(2 \lambda+1)-3(-3 \lambda+1)+2(2 \lambda+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{17} \end{aligned} $$

So $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \equiv\left(\frac{-2}{17}+1, \frac{3}{17}+1, \frac{-2}{17}+1\right)$

$$ \Rightarrow 34(a+b+c)=100 $$

questions.questionText:
The number of critical points of the function

$f(x) = \begin{cases} |\frac{\sin x}{x}|, & x \neq 0 \\ 1, & x = 0 \end{cases}$ in the interval $(-2\pi, 2\pi)$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
First check continuity at $x=0$:

$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left|\frac{\sin x}{x}\right|=1=f(0) \rightarrow f(x)$ is continuous

For $x \neq 0$, write $g(x)=\frac{\sin x}{x}$. Then

Now $\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)=\frac{x \cos x-\sin x}{x^2}$

To find critical points, we look for points in $(-2\pi,2\pi)$ where either $f'(x)=0$ or $f'(x)$ does not exist.

At points where $\frac{\sin x}{x} \neq 0$, the absolute value does not create a corner, so we can use $g'(x)=0$ to get stationary points:

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=0 \Rightarrow \tan \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{x}$

The equation $\tan x=x$ has 3 solutions in $(-2\pi,2\pi)$ (one is $x=0$, and one each in $(\pi, \tfrac{3\pi}{2})$ and $(-\tfrac{3\pi}{2},-\pi)$):

Next, $f'(x)$ does not exist at the zeros of $\left|\frac{\sin x}{x}\right|$, because $|\,\cdot\,|$ creates a sharp corner when the inside changes sign. Here $\sin x=0$ gives $x=\pm \pi$ (and $\pm 2\pi$ etc.). In the open interval $(-2\pi,2\pi)$, only $x=-\pi$ and $x=\pi$ lie inside.

So we have $3$ critical points from $\tan x=x$, and $2$ more critical points where $f'(x)$ does not exist at $x=-\pi,\pi$.

total 5 points

questions.questionText: Let $x$ and $y$ be real numbers such that $50\left(\frac{2 x}{1+3 i}-\frac{y}{1-2 i}\right)=31+17 i, i=\sqrt{-1}$. Then the value of $10(x-3 y)$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$ 50\left(\frac{2x}{1+3i}-\frac{y}{1-2i}\right)=31+17i $

We need to find $10(x-3y)$.

First, simplify the fractions by rationalising the denominators.

$ \frac{2x}{1+3i}=\frac{2x(1-3i)}{(1+3i)(1-3i)} $

$ =\frac{2x(1-3i)}{1+9}=\frac{2x(1-3i)}{10} $

$ =\frac{x}{5}(1-3i) $

Similarly,

$ \frac{y}{1-2i}=\frac{y(1+2i)}{(1-2i)(1+2i)} $

$ =\frac{y(1+2i)}{1+4}=\frac{y(1+2i)}{5} $

Now substitute into the given equation:

$ 50\left(\frac{x}{5}(1-3i)-\frac{y}{5}(1+2i)\right)=31+17i $

Take $\frac{1}{5}$ common inside:

$ 50\cdot \frac{1}{5}\left[x(1-3i)-y(1+2i)\right]=31+17i $

$ 10\left[x(1-3i)-y(1+2i)\right]=31+17i $

Expand:

$ 10\left[x-3xi-y-2yi\right]=31+17i $

$ 10\left[(x-y)+(-3x-2y)i\right]=31+17i $

So,

$ 10(x-y)+10(-3x-2y)i=31+17i $

Now compare real and imaginary parts:

$ 10(x-y)=31 $

$ 10(-3x-2y)=17 $

Thus,

$ x-y=\frac{31}{10} $

$ -3x-2y=\frac{17}{10} $

Now solve these equations.

From the first equation,

$ x=y+\frac{31}{10} $

Substitute into the second:

$ -3\left(y+\frac{31}{10}\right)-2y=\frac{17}{10} $

$ -3y-\frac{93}{10}-2y=\frac{17}{10} $

$ -5y=\frac{17}{10}+\frac{93}{10} $

$ -5y=\frac{110}{10}=11 $

$ y=-\frac{11}{5} $

Then

$ x=y+\frac{31}{10}=-\frac{11}{5}+\frac{31}{10} $

$ x=-\frac{22}{10}+\frac{31}{10}=\frac{9}{10} $

Now find:

$ 10(x-3y)=10\left(\frac{9}{10}-3\left(-\frac{11}{5}\right)\right) $

$ =10\left(\frac{9}{10}+\frac{33}{5}\right) $

$ =10\left(\frac{9}{10}+\frac{66}{10}\right) $

$ =10\cdot \frac{75}{10}=75 $

Therefore, the value of $10(x-3y)$ is

$ \boxed{75} $

So, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

questions.questionText: Let [.] denote the greatest integer function. Then the value of $\int\limits_{0}^{3} \left( \frac{e^{x} + e^{-x}}{[x]!} \right) dx$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.questionText: If the curve $y = f(x)$ passes through the point $(1, e)$ and satisfies the differential equation $dy = y(2 + \\log_e x) dx$, $x > 0$, then $f(e)$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = y(2+\ln x)$$

To solve this, first separate the variables by dividing both sides by $y$:

$$\int\limits{} \frac{dy}{y} = \int\limits{} (2+\ln x)\,dx$$

Now integrate both sides. The left side gives $\ln y$. For the right side, integrate term by term:

$$\ln y = 2x + x\ln x - x + C$$

Combine like terms $2x-x=x$:

$$\ln y = x + x\ln x + C$$

Since it passes through $(1,e)$, put $x=1$ and $y=e$ in the equation. Then $\ln y=\ln e=1$ and $\ln 1=0$:

$$1 = 1 + 0 + C \rightarrow C = 0$$

So the required relation becomes:

$$\ln y = x + x\ln x$$

Taking antilog (exponential) on both sides:

$$f(x)=y=e^{x+x\ln x}$$

Now substitute $x=e$. Also, $\ln e=1$:

$$\Rightarrow f(e)=e^{e+e}=e^{2e}$$

questions.questionText:
Let $y = y(x)$ be the solution curve of the differential equation

$(1 + \sin x)\dfrac{dy}{dx} + (y + 1)\cos x = 0,\ y(0) = 0.$ If the curve $y = y(x)$ passes through the point $\left( \alpha , \dfrac{-1}{2} \right)$,

then a value of $\alpha$ is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\int \frac{dy}{y+1}=-\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\,dx$$

On the left side, $\int \frac{dy}{y+1}=\ln (y+1)$.

On the right side, take $1+\sin x$ as substitution: let $u=1+\sin x$, then $du=\cos x\,dx$. So, $$-\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\,dx=-\int \frac{du}{u}=-\ln(1+\sin x).$$

So we get $$\Rightarrow \ln (y+1)=-\ln (1+\sin x)+C$$

Now use the initial condition $y(0)=0$. Put $x=0$ and $y=0$ in the equation:

$$\therefore y(0)=0 \rightarrow \ln(0+1)=-\ln(1+\sin 0)+C$$

That is $$0=-\ln(1)+C \Rightarrow C=0.$$

So $$\Rightarrow \ln (y+1)=-\ln (1+\sin x)$$

Taking antilog on both sides, $$\Rightarrow y+1=\frac{1}{1+\sin x}$$

Given that the curve passes through $$\left(\alpha,-\frac{1}{2}\right)$$, substitute $y=-\frac{1}{2}$ and $x=\alpha$:

$$-\frac{1}{2}+1=\frac{1}{1+\sin \alpha}$$

So $$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{1+\sin \alpha} \Rightarrow 1+\sin \alpha=2 \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=1$$

Now $\sin \alpha=1$ gives $$\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ (as per options)}$$

questions.questionText:
Let an ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$, $a < b$, pass through the point (4, 3) and have eccentricity $\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}$.

Then the length of its latus rectum is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since $a < b$, the major axis is along the $y$-axis. So for the ellipse

$$ \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1, $$

we use the eccentricity formula

$$ e=\sqrt{1-\frac{a^2}{b^2}}. $$

Given

$$ e=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}, $$

so

$$ e^2=\frac{5}{9}. $$

Hence,

$$ 1-\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{5}{9} $$

which gives

$$ \frac{a^2}{b^2}=1-\frac{5}{9}=\frac{4}{9}. $$

Therefore,

$$ a^2=\frac{4}{9}b^2. $$

Now the ellipse passes through $(4,3)$, so it satisfies the equation:

$$ \frac{4^2}{a^2}+\frac{3^2}{b^2}=1. $$

That is,

$$ \frac{16}{a^2}+\frac{9}{b^2}=1. $$

Substitute $a^2=\frac{4}{9}b^2$:

$$ \frac{16}{\frac{4}{9}b^2}+\frac{9}{b^2}=1. $$

Simplifying,

$$ 16\cdot \frac{9}{4b^2}+\frac{9}{b^2}=1 $$

$$ \frac{36}{b^2}+\frac{9}{b^2}=1 $$

$$ \frac{45}{b^2}=1 $$

so

$$ b^2=45. $$

Then

$$ a^2=\frac{4}{9}\cdot 45=20. $$

For an ellipse with major axis along the $y$-axis, the length of latus rectum is

$$ \frac{2a^2}{b}. $$

So,

$$ \text{Length of latus rectum}=\frac{2\cdot 20}{\sqrt{45}} =\frac{40}{3\sqrt{5}}. $$

Rationalising,

$$ \frac{40}{3\sqrt{5}}\cdot \frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{5}} =\frac{8\sqrt{5}}{3}. $$

Therefore, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{8\sqrt{5}}{3}} $$

So, Option D is correct.

questions.questionText: If $ \lim\limits_{x\to 2} \frac{\sin\left(x^3 - 5x^2 + ax + b\right)}{\left(\sqrt{x-1}-1\right) \log_e(x-1)} = m $, then $a + b + m$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\therefore$ Denominator $=0$ at $x=2$

$\Rightarrow$ Numerator $=0$ at $x=2$

$$2^3-5(2)^2+a(2)+b=0$$

$$\Rightarrow 2a+b=12 \quad \ldots (1)$$

$$\therefore \; m=\lim\limits_{x\rightarrow 2}\frac{\dfrac{\sin\left(x^3-5x^2+ax+b\right)}{x^3-5x^2+ax+b}\cdot \left(x^3-5x^2+ax+b\right)}{\left(\dfrac{x-1-1}{\sqrt{x-1}+1}\right)\cdot \dfrac{\log_e\left(1+(x-2)\right)}{x-2}\cdot (x-2)}$$

$$\Rightarrow m=\lim\limits_{x\rightarrow 2} 2\left(\frac{x^3-5x^2+ax+b}{(x-2)^2}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow m=\lim\limits_{x\rightarrow 2} 2\left(\frac{3x^2-10x+a}{2(x-2)}\right)$$

$\therefore$ Denominator $=0$ at $x=2$

$\Rightarrow$ Numerator $=0$ at $x=2$

$$\Rightarrow 3(2)^2-10(2)+a=0 \rightarrow a=8$$

put $a=8$ in (1), we get $b=-4$

$$\therefore \; m=\lim\limits_{x\rightarrow 2} 2\left(\frac{6x-10}{2}\right) \rightarrow m=2$$

$\therefore \; a+b+m=6$

questions.questionText:
Let $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$ be such that the system of linear equations

$ \begin{aligned} x + 2y + z &= 5 \\ 2x + y + \alpha z &= 5 \\ 8x + 4y + \beta z &= 18 \end{aligned} $

has no solution. Then $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x+2 y+z=5 \\ & 2 x+y+\alpha z=5 \\ & 8 x+4 y+\beta z=18 \end{aligned} $$

For a system of three linear equations to have no solution, it is necessary that the coefficient matrix be singular, i.e. its determinant is zero: $\,\Delta=0$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & \alpha \\ 8 & 4 & \beta \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Delta=1(\beta-4 \alpha)-2(2 \beta-8 \alpha)+1(0)=0 \\ & =\beta-4 \alpha-4 \beta+16 \alpha=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \alpha=\beta \end{aligned} $$

Now substitute $\beta=4\alpha$ in the third equation. Then the left side of the third equation becomes $8x+4y+4\alpha z$, which is exactly $4$ times the left side of the second equation $2x+y+\alpha z$.

But the constants do not match: $4\times 5=20$, whereas the third equation has $18$. So we get $4\times(2x+y+\alpha z)=20$ but $8x+4y+4\alpha z=18$, which is a contradiction. Hence, the system is inconsistent and has no solution.

Therefore, $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}=\dfrac{4\alpha}{\alpha}=4$.

questions.questionText:
Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & \alpha \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ \beta & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. If $A^2 - 4A + I = O$ and $B^2 - 5B - 6I = O$, then among the two statements:

(S1) : $[(B-A)(B+A)]^T = \begin{bmatrix} 13 & 15 \\ 7 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$

and

(S2) : $\det(\mathrm{adj}(A+B)) = -5$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & \alpha \end{array}\right] \\ & \mathrm{A}^2-4 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I}=0 \end{aligned} $$

trace of matrix

$$ \begin{aligned} & \alpha+1=4 \\ & \alpha=3 \\ & \mathrm{~B}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 3 \\ \beta & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & \mathrm{B}^2-5 \mathrm{~B}-6 \mathrm{I}=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\begin{aligned} & \operatorname{det} .(B)=6-3 \beta=-6 \\ & 3 \beta=12 \\ & \beta=4 \\ & B-A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1\end{array}\right] ; B+A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}4 & 5 \\ 5 & 5\end{array}\right] \\ & (B-A)(B+A)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}13 & 15 \\ 7 & 10\end{array}\right] \\ & \operatorname{adj}(A)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}3 & -2 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right], \operatorname{adjB}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -3 \\ -4 & 3\end{array}\right] \\ & \operatorname{adj}(A+B)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}5 & -5 \\ -5 & 4\end{array}\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.questionText: The number of seven-digit numbers, that can be formed by using the digits 1, 2, 3, 5 and 7 such that each digit is used at least once, is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have to form a $7$-digit number using the digits $1,2,3,5,7$ such that each digit is used at least once.

Since there are $5$ distinct digits and the number has $7$ places, after using each digit once, there are $2$ extra places.

So, the repetition can happen in the following ways:

Case 1: One digit is repeated $3$ times

Then the distribution of digits is:

$$3,1,1,1,1$$

Choose the digit which appears $3$ times:

$$^5C_1 = 5$$

Now arrange the $7$ digits:

$$\frac{7!}{3!}$$

So, total numbers in this case:

$$5 \times \frac{7!}{3!} = 5 \times \frac{5040}{6} = 5 \times 840 = 4200$$

Case 2: Two digits are repeated $2$ times each

Then the distribution is:

$$2,2,1,1,1$$

Choose the two digits which are repeated:

$$^5C_2 = 10$$

Now arrange the $7$ digits:

$$\frac{7!}{2!2!}$$

So, total numbers in this case:

$$10 \times \frac{7!}{2!2!} = 10 \times \frac{5040}{4} = 10 \times 1260 = 12600$$

Total number of such $7$-digit numbers

$$4200 + 12600 = 16800$$

Hence, the required number is

$$\boxed{16800}$$

So, the correct option is Option C.

questions.questionText: The number of elements in the set $S = \left\{ (r, k) : k \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ and } ^{36}C_{r+1} = \frac{6\left(^{35}C_{r}\right)}{(k^2-3)} \right\}$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need to find the number of ordered pairs $(r,k)$ satisfying

$$ {}^{36}C_{r+1}=\frac{6\left({}^{35}C_r\right)}{k^2-3}, $$

where $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

Let us simplify the relation using the standard NCERT combination formula.

We know that

$$ {}^{n}C_{r+1}=\frac{n!}{(r+1)!(n-r-1)!} $$

and

$$ {}^{n-1}C_r=\frac{(n-1)!}{r!(n-r-1)!}. $$

So,

$$ \frac{{}^{36}C_{r+1}}{{}^{35}C_r} = \frac{\dfrac{36!}{(r+1)!(35-r)!}}{\dfrac{35!}{r!(35-r)!}} = \frac{36!}{35!}\cdot \frac{r!}{(r+1)!} = 36\cdot \frac{1}{r+1} = \frac{36}{r+1}. $$

Hence,

$$ {}^{36}C_{r+1} = {}^{35}C_r \cdot \frac{36}{r+1}. $$

Given,

$$ {}^{36}C_{r+1}=\frac{6\left({}^{35}C_r\right)}{k^2-3}. $$

Comparing both expressions,

$$ {}^{35}C_r \cdot \frac{36}{r+1}=\frac{6\left({}^{35}C_r\right)}{k^2-3}. $$

Since ${}^{35}C_r \neq 0$, cancel it:

$$ \frac{36}{r+1}=\frac{6}{k^2-3}. $$

Cross-multiplying,

$$ 36(k^2-3)=6(r+1). $$

Divide by $6$:

$$ 6(k^2-3)=r+1. $$

So,

$$ r = 6k^2-19. $$

Now $r$ must be valid for the combinations ${}^{35}C_r$ and ${}^{36}C_{r+1}$.

Thus,

$$ 0 \le r \le 35. $$

Substitute $r=6k^2-19$:

$$ 0 \le 6k^2-19 \le 35. $$

Add $19$ throughout:

$$ 19 \le 6k^2 \le 54. $$

Divide by $6$:

$$ \frac{19}{6} \le k^2 \le 9. $$

Now possible integer square values are

$$ k^2=4 \quad \text{or} \quad k^2=9. $$

So the integer values of $k$ are:

  • If $k^2=4$, then $k=\pm 2$

  • If $k^2=9$, then $k=\pm 3$

Now find corresponding $r$:

For $k^2=4$,

$$ r=6(4)-19=24-19=5. $$

So pairs are

$$ (5,2),\ (5,-2). $$

For $k^2=9$,

$$ r=6(9)-19=54-19=35. $$

So pairs are

$$ (35,3),\ (35,-3). $$

Hence total number of elements in the set $S$ is

$$ 4. $$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{4} $$

So, Option B is correct.

questions.questionText: Let $\alpha, \alpha+2, \alpha \in \mathbf{Z}$, be the roots of the quadratic equation $x(x+2)+(x+1)(x+3)+(x+2)(x+4)+\ldots .+(x+\mathrm{n}-1)(x+\mathrm{n}+1)=4 \mathrm{n}$ for some $\mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{N}$. Then $\mathrm{n}+\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We are given that

the quadratic equation is

$ (x)(x+2) + (x+1)(x+3) + (x+2)(x+4) + \ldots + (x + n - 1)(x + n + 1) = 4n $

and the roots are $ \alpha, \alpha + 2 $ (both integers).

We need to find the value of $ n + \alpha $.

Step 1: Expand the general term

The $k^{th}$ term of the left-hand side is:

$ (x + k)(x + k + 2) $

Expanding this:

$ (x + k)(x + k + 2) = x^2 + 2(k+1)x + k(k+2) $

Step 2: Sum over all $k$ from $0$ to $n-1$

$ \text{LHS} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} [x^2 + 2(k+1)x + k(k+2)] $

Let's compute each part separately.

  1. For $x^2$:

    $ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x^2 = n x^2 $

  2. For $2(k+1)x$:

    $ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 2(k+1)x = 2x \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (k+1) = 2x \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = n(n+1)x $

  3. For $k(k+2)$:

    $ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k(k+2) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (k^2 + 2k) = \sum k^2 + 2\sum k $

Now,

$ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}, \quad \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k^2 = \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} $

Therefore,

$ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k(k+2) = \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} + n(n-1) $

Simplify:

$ = n(n-1)\left( \frac{2n-1}{6} + 1 \right) = n(n-1)\left( \frac{2n-1+6}{6} \right) = \frac{n(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} $

Step 3: Combine all terms

$ \text{LHS} = n x^2 + n(n+1)x + \frac{n(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} $

The equation is

$ n x^2 + n(n+1)x + \frac{n(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} = 4n $

Step 4: Simplify by dividing both sides by $n$ (since $n > 0$)

$ x^2 + (n+1)x + \frac{(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} = 4 $

$ x^2 + (n+1)x + \left[\frac{(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} - 4\right] = 0 $

Simplify the constant term:

$ \frac{(n-1)(2n+5)}{6} - 4 = \frac{(n-1)(2n+5) - 24}{6} = \frac{2n^2 + 3n -29}{6} $

Thus, the equation is

$ x^2 + (n+1)x + \frac{2n^2 + 3n - 29}{6} = 0 $

Step 5: Use relation between roots and coefficients

Let roots be $ \alpha $ and $ \alpha + 2 $.

Then,

Sum of roots:

$ \alpha + (\alpha + 2) = 2\alpha + 2 = - (n+1) $

so,

$ \alpha = \frac{ - (n+1) - 2}{2} = -\frac{n+3}{2} $

Product of roots:

$ \alpha(\alpha + 2) = \frac{2n^2 + 3n - 29}{6} $

Substitute $ \alpha = -\frac{n+3}{2} $:

$ \alpha(\alpha + 2) = \left(-\frac{n+3}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{n-1}{2}\right) = \frac{(n+3)(n-1)}{4} $

Step 6: Equate the two expressions for product

$ \frac{(n+3)(n-1)}{4} = \frac{2n^2 + 3n - 29}{6} $

Simplify:

$ 3(n+3)(n-1) = 2(2n^2 + 3n - 29) $

Expand:

$ 3(n^2 + 2n - 3) = 4n^2 + 6n - 58 $

$ 3n^2 + 6n - 9 = 4n^2 + 6n - 58 $

Simplify:

$ 3n^2 + 6n - 9 - 4n^2 - 6n + 58 = 0 $

$ -n^2 + 49 = 0 \implies n^2 = 49 $

$ n = 7 \text{ (since } n \in \mathbf{N}) $

Step 7: Find $ \alpha $ and hence $ n + \alpha $

$ \alpha = -\frac{n+3}{2} = -\frac{7+3}{2} = -5 $

$ n + \alpha = 7 + (-5) = 2 $

$ \boxed{n + \alpha = 2} $

Correct Option: C (2)

questions.questionText: Let A be the set of first 101 terms of an A.P., whose first term is 1 and the common difference is 5 and let B be the set of first 71 terms of an A.P., whose first term is 9 and the common difference is 7. Then the number of elements in $A \cap B$, which are divisible by 3, is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Understand Set A

Set $A$ consists of the first 101 terms of an A.P. with the first term $a = 1$ and common difference $d_1 = 5$.

The $n$-th term of this A.P. is given by the formula:

$$a_n = a + (n - 1)d_1$$

Therefore, the last term (101st term) of Set A is:

$$a_{101} = 1 + (101 - 1) \times 5 = 1 + 100 \times 5 = 501$$

So, $A = \{1, 6, 11, 16, 21, \dots, 501 \}$.

Understand Set B

Set $B$ consists of the first 71 terms of an A.P. with the first term $b = 9$ and common difference $d_2 = 7$.

The $m$-th term of this A.P. is:

$$b_m = b + (m - 1)d_2$$

Therefore, the last term (71st term) of Set B is:

$$b_{71} = 9 + (71 - 1) \times 7 = 9 + 70 \times 7 = 499$$

So, $B = \{9, 16, 23, 30, \dots, 499 \}$.

Find the elements in $A \cap B$

The elements common to both sets $A$ and $B$ will themselves form an A.P.

From the initial terms listed above, we can easily see that the first common term is $16$.

The common difference of this new A.P. will be the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of the common differences of the two original sets:

$$\text{Common difference of } A \cap B = \text{LCM}(5, 7) = 35$$

The $k$-th term of the common sequence is given by:

$$C_k = 16 + (k - 1)35$$

Since a common term must exist in both sets, it cannot be greater than the smallest of their last terms. Thus, $C_k \le \text{min}(501, 499)$.

$$16 + (k - 1)35 \le 499$$

$$35(k - 1) \le 483$$

$$k - 1 \le \frac{483}{35}$$

$$k - 1 \le 13.8$$

Since $k$ must be a positive integer, the maximum possible value for $(k - 1)$ is $13$. Thus, $k \le 14$.

There are altogether 14 common terms in $A \cap B$.

Find elements in $A \cap B$ divisible by 3

We want to find terms $C_k$ that are divisible by $3$, which means:

$$C_k \equiv 0 \pmod 3$$

$$16 + 35(k - 1) \equiv 0 \pmod 3$$

Since $16 = 3 \times 5 + 1 \implies 16 \equiv 1 \pmod 3$ and $35 = 3 \times 11 + 2 \implies 35 \equiv 2 \pmod 3$, we can simplify:

$$1 + 2(k - 1) \equiv 0 \pmod 3$$

Checking the first few integer values for $k$:

  • If $k = 1$: $1 + 2(0) = 1$ (Not divisible by 3)

  • If $k = 2$: $1 + 2(1) = 3 \equiv 0 \pmod 3$ (Divisible by 3)

  • If $k = 3$: $1 + 2(2) = 5 \equiv 2 \pmod 3$ (Not divisible by 3)

The condition holds true when $k = 2$. Because we are operating in modulo $3$, the values of $k$ that satisfy this condition will form an A.P. with a common difference of $3$.

Thus, the valid values of $k$ within the range $1 \le k \le 14$ are:

$$k \in \{2, 5, 8, 11, 14\}$$

Counting these, there are exactly $5$ valid values of $k$.

For confirmation, the terms are:

  • For $k = 2 \rightarrow 51$

  • For $k = 5 \rightarrow 156$

  • For $k = 8 \rightarrow 261$

  • For $k = 11 \rightarrow 366$

  • For $k = 14 \rightarrow 471$

All 5 of these terms are common elements between the sets and are completely divisible by 3.

Correct Answer:

Option B

questions.questionText: Let the mid points of the sides of a triangle ABC be $\left(\frac{5}{2}, 7\right)$, $\left(\frac{5}{2}, 3\right)$ and $(4, 5)$. If its incentre is $(h, k)$, then $3h + k$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let the midpoints of the sides of triangle $ABC$ be

$$ D\left(\frac{5}{2}, 7\right), \quad E\left(\frac{5}{2}, 3\right), \quad F(4, 5) $$

We have to find the incentre $(h,k)$ of triangle $ABC$.

Step 1: Use the fact about the medial triangle

The triangle formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of a triangle is called the medial triangle.

If the midpoints are known, then the original triangle can be reconstructed using:

$$ A = E + F - D,\quad B = F + D - E,\quad C = D + E - F $$

Now calculate each vertex.

Vertex $A$

$$ A = \left(\frac{5}{2}, 3\right) + (4, 5) - \left(\frac{5}{2}, 7\right) $$

$$ A = (4, 1) $$

Vertex $B$

$$ B = (4, 5) + \left(\frac{5}{2}, 7\right) - \left(\frac{5}{2}, 3\right) $$

$$ B = (4, 9) $$

Vertex $C$

$$ C = \left(\frac{5}{2}, 7\right) + \left(\frac{5}{2}, 3\right) - (4, 5) $$

$$ C = (1, 5) $$

So the triangle has vertices

$$ A(4,1),\quad B(4,9),\quad C(1,5) $$

Step 2: Find the side lengths

Using distance formula:

Side opposite $A$, i.e. $BC$

$$ BC = \sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (9-5)^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5 $$

Side opposite $B$, i.e. $CA$

$$ CA = \sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + (-4)^2} = 5 $$

Side opposite $C$, i.e. $AB$

$$ AB = \sqrt{(4-4)^2 + (9-1)^2} = 8 $$

Thus,

$$ a = 5,\quad b = 5,\quad c = 8 $$

Step 3: Use incentre formula

The incentre of triangle with vertices $A(x_1,y_1)$, $B(x_2,y_2)$, $C(x_3,y_3)$ is

$$ \left( \frac{ax_1 + bx_2 + cx_3}{a+b+c}, \frac{ay_1 + by_2 + cy_3}{a+b+c} \right) $$

where

  • $a = BC$

  • $b = CA$

  • $c = AB$

Substitute the values:

$$ h = \frac{5\cdot 4 + 5\cdot 4 + 8\cdot 1}{5+5+8} = \frac{20+20+8}{18} = \frac{48}{18} = \frac{8}{3} $$

$$ k = \frac{5\cdot 1 + 5\cdot 9 + 8\cdot 5}{18} = \frac{5+45+40}{18} = \frac{90}{18} = 5 $$

So the incentre is

$$ \left(\frac{8}{3}, 5\right) $$

Step 4: Find $3h + k$

$$ 3h + k = 3\cdot \frac{8}{3} + 5 = 8 + 5 = 13 $$

Therefore, the answer is

$$ \boxed{13} $$

So, the correct option is Option C.

questions.questionText: Let $S = \{x \in [-\pi, \pi] : \sin x (\sin x + \cos x) = a,\ a \in \mathbb{Z} \}$. Then $n(S)$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need to find the number of elements in

$$ S=\{x\in[-\pi,\pi]: \sin x(\sin x+\cos x)=a,\; a\in\mathbb Z\}. $$

So we must find all $x\in[-\pi,\pi]$ for which the value

$$ \sin x(\sin x+\cos x) $$

is an integer.

Step 1: Simplify the expression

Let

$$ f(x)=\sin x(\sin x+\cos x). $$

Then

$$ f(x)=\sin^2 x+\sin x\cos x. $$

Now use the identities:

$$ \sin^2 x=\frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}, \qquad \sin x\cos x=\frac{\sin 2x}{2}. $$

So,

$$ f(x)=\frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}+\frac{\sin 2x}{2} = \frac{1+\sin 2x-\cos 2x}{2}. $$

Also,

$$ \sin 2x-\cos 2x=\sqrt{2}\sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right). $$

Hence,

$$ f(x)=\frac{1+\sqrt{2}\sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{2}. $$

Step 2: Find the range of $f(x)$

Since

$$ -1\le \sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\le 1, $$

we get

$$ -\sqrt{2}\le \sqrt{2}\sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\le \sqrt{2}. $$

Therefore,

$$ \frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{2}\le f(x)\le \frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{2}. $$

Now,

$$ \frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{2}\approx -0.207,\qquad \frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{2}\approx 1.207. $$

So the only integers possible in this interval are

$$ 0 \quad \text{and} \quad 1. $$

Thus we need to solve:

  1. $f(x)=0$

  2. $f(x)=1$

Step 3: Solve $f(x)=0$

We have

$$ \sin x(\sin x+\cos x)=0. $$

So either

Case 1:

$$ \sin x=0 $$

In $[-\pi,\pi]$, this gives

$$ x=-\pi,\;0,\;\pi. $$

Case 2:

$$ \sin x+\cos x=0 $$

That is,

$$ \sqrt{2}\sin\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=0 $$

so

$$ x+\frac{\pi}{4}=n\pi \quad\Rightarrow\quad x=n\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}. $$

Now check in $[-\pi,\pi]$:

  • for $n=0$, $x=-\frac{\pi}{4}$

  • for $n=1$, $x=\frac{3\pi}{4}$

  • other values go outside the interval

So from $f(x)=0$, we get

$$ x=-\pi,\;0,\;\pi,\;-\frac{\pi}{4},\;\frac{3\pi}{4}. $$

Total $5$ values.

Step 4: Solve $f(x)=1$

We need

$$ \sin x(\sin x+\cos x)=1. $$

Using the simplified form:

$$ \frac{1+\sqrt{2}\sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{2}=1. $$

So,

$$ 1+\sqrt{2}\sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2 $$

$$ \sqrt{2}\sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=1 $$

$$ \sin\left(2x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}. $$

Now,

$$ \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

when

$$ \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+2n\pi \quad\text{or}\quad \theta=\frac{3\pi}{4}+2n\pi. $$

So,

First possibility:

$$ 2x-\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}+2n\pi $$

$$ 2x=\frac{\pi}{2}+2n\pi $$

$$ x=\frac{\pi}{4}+n\pi $$

In $[-\pi,\pi]$, this gives

$$ x=\frac{\pi}{4},\;-\frac{3\pi}{4}. $$

Second possibility:

$$ 2x-\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{3\pi}{4}+2n\pi $$

$$ 2x=\pi+2n\pi $$

$$ x=\frac{\pi}{2}+n\pi $$

In $[-\pi,\pi]$, this gives

$$ x=\frac{\pi}{2},\;-\frac{\pi}{2}. $$

So from $f(x)=1$, we get $4$ values:

$$ x=\frac{\pi}{4},\;-\frac{3\pi}{4},\;\frac{\pi}{2},\;-\frac{\pi}{2}. $$

Step 5: Total number of elements

Combining both cases:

  • from $a=0$: $5$ values

  • from $a=1$: $4$ values

Hence,

$$ n(S)=5+4=9. $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{9} $$

Therefore, Option D is correct.

questions.questionText: If $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\frac{7\pi}{18}\right) = K$, then the value of $\sin\left(\frac{10K\pi}{3}\right)$ is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let

$$ K=\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{18}\right)\sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{18}\right)\sin\left(\frac{7\pi}{18}\right) $$

We need to find

$$ \sin\left(\frac{10K\pi}{3}\right) $$

First, write the angles in degrees:

$$ \frac{\pi}{18}=10^\circ,\qquad \frac{5\pi}{18}=50^\circ,\qquad \frac{7\pi}{18}=70^\circ $$

So,

$$ K=\sin 10^\circ \sin 50^\circ \sin 70^\circ $$

Now use the standard identity:

$$ \sin 3x = 4\sin x \sin(60^\circ+x)\sin(60^\circ-x) $$

Taking $x=10^\circ$,

$$ \sin 30^\circ = 4\sin 10^\circ \sin 70^\circ \sin 50^\circ $$

Since

$$ \sin 30^\circ=\frac12 $$

we get

$$ \frac12 = 4\sin 10^\circ \sin 50^\circ \sin 70^\circ $$

Hence,

$$ \sin 10^\circ \sin 50^\circ \sin 70^\circ = \frac{1}{8} $$

Therefore,

$$ K=\frac18 $$

Now substitute in the required expression:

$$ \sin\left(\frac{10K\pi}{3}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{10}{3}\cdot\frac18\pi\right) = \sin\left(\frac{10\pi}{24}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{12}\right) $$

Now,

$$ \frac{5\pi}{12}=75^\circ $$

So,

$$ \sin 75^\circ=\sin(45^\circ+30^\circ) $$

Using the formula $\sin(A+B)$:

$$ \sin 75^\circ=\sin 45^\circ \cos 30^\circ+\cos 45^\circ \sin 30^\circ $$

$$ =\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt3}{2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\cdot\frac12 $$

$$ =\frac{\sqrt3+1}{2\sqrt2} $$

So the value is

$$ \boxed{\frac{\sqrt3+1}{2\sqrt2}} $$

Hence, the correct option is A.

questions.questionText: The velocity of a particle is given as $\vec{v} = -x \hat{i} + 2y \hat{j} - z \hat{k}$ m/s. The magnitude of acceleration at point (1, 2, 4) is ________ m/s2.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ is the total derivative of velocity with respect to time:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$$

The velocity of the particle is given as $\vec{v} = -x\hat{i} + 2y\hat{j} - z\hat{k}\ \mathrm{m/s}$

The x-component of velocity is $v_x = -x$

So, the x-component of acceleration is $a_x = \frac{dv_x}{dt} = \frac{dv_x}{dx} \times \frac{dx}{dt} = v_x\frac{dv_x}{dx} = (-x)\frac{d(-x)}{dx} = x$

The y-component of velocity is $v_y = 2y$

So, the y-component of acceleration is $a_y = \frac{dv_y}{dt} = \frac{dv_y}{dy} \times \frac{dy}{dt} = v_y\frac{dv_y}{dx} = (2y)\frac{d(2y)}{dx} = (2y)(2) = 4y$

The z-component of velocity is $v_z = -z$

So, the z-component of acceleration is $a_z = \frac{dv_z}{dt} = \frac{dv_z}{dy} \times \frac{dz}{dt} = v_z\frac{dv_z}{dx} = (-z)\frac{d(-z)}{dx} = (-z)(-1) = z$

Thus, the acceleration vector is $\vec{a} = x\hat{i} + 4y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$.

At point $(1,2,4)$:

$$\vec{a} = (1)\hat{i} + 4(2)\hat{j} + (4)\hat{k} = \hat{i} + 8\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$$

The magnitude $|\vec{a}|$ is,

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$$

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{1^2 + 8^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt{81}$$

$$|\vec{a}| = 9\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Therefore, the magnitude of acceleration at the given point is $9\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$. Therefore, the correct option is (B).

questions.questionText: Let a circle C have its centre in the first quadrant, intersect the coordinate axes at exactly three points and cut off equal intercepts from the coordinate axes. If the length of the chord of C on the line $x + y = 1$ is $\sqrt{14}$, then the square of the radius of C is ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & r^2=\left(\frac{\sqrt{14}}{2}\right)^2+\left(r-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \\ & r^2=\frac{7}{2}+r^2-\sqrt{2} r+\frac{1}{2} \\ & \sqrt{2} r=4 \\ & r=2 \sqrt{2} \\ & r^2=8\end{aligned}$

questions.questionText: If $\alpha = \int\limits_{0}^{2\sqrt{3}} \log_{2}(x^{2} + 4) \, dx + \int\limits_{2}^{4} \sqrt{2x - 4} \, dx$, then $\alpha^{2}$ is equal to ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
As we know that

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}+\int_{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})}^{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~b})} \mathrm{f}^{-1}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{bf}(\mathrm{~b})-\mathrm{af}(\mathrm{a}) \\ & \therefore \alpha=(2 \sqrt{3})(4)-(0)(2) \\ & =8 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha^2=192 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText:
If the domain of the function

$f(x) = \sqrt{\log_{(0.6)} (\left| \frac{2x-5}{x^2-4} \right|)}$ is $(-\infty, a] \cup \{b\} \cup [c, d) \cup (e, \infty)$, then the value of $a + b + c + d + e$ is ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For domain $\log _{0.6}\left|\frac{2 x-5}{x^2-4}\right| \geq 0$

$$\left|\frac{2x-5}{x^2-4}\right| \leq 1 \quad \& \quad x \neq \frac{5}{2} \qquad \ldots..\,(1)$$

$$-1 \leq \frac{2x-5}{x^2-4} \leq 1$$

$$\frac{2x-5}{x^2-4} + 1 \geq 0$$

$$\frac{x^2+2x-9}{x^2-4} \geq 0$$

$$\frac{(x+1)^2-10}{(x-2)(x+2)} \geq 0$$

$$x \in \left(-\infty, -1-\sqrt{10}\right] \cup (-2,2) \cup \left[-1+\sqrt{10}, \infty\right) \qquad \ldots..(2)$$

$$\frac{2x-5}{x^2-4} - 1 \leq 0$$

$$\frac{2x-5-x^2+4}{x^2-4} \leq 0$$

$$\frac{x^2-2x+1}{x^2-4} \geq 0$$

$$\frac{(x-1)^2}{(x-2)(x+2)} \geq 0$$

$$x \in (-\infty,-2) \cup (2,\infty) \cup \{1\} \qquad \ldots..(3)$$

$$(1) \cap (2) \cap (3)$$

$$x \in \left(-\infty,-1-\sqrt{10}\right] \cup \{1\} \cup \left[-1+\sqrt{10}, \frac{5}{2}\right) \cup \left(\frac{5}{2}, \infty\right)$$

$$a+b+c+d+e=-2+1+5=4$$

questions.questionText: Let a, b, c ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4}. If the probability, that $a x^2 + 2\sqrt{2} bx + c > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, is $\frac{m}{n}$, $\gcd(m, n) = 1$, then $m + n$ is equal to ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the quadratic expression

$$ ax^2 + 2\sqrt{2}\,bx + c $$

to be positive for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, we use the NCERT condition for a quadratic $Ax^2+Bx+C$ to satisfy

$$ Ax^2+Bx+C > 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbb{R}, $$

which is:

  1. $A>0$

  2. Discriminant $<0$

Here,

$$ A=a,\quad B=2\sqrt{2}\,b,\quad C=c $$

Since $a \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, we always have

$$ a>0 $$

So we only need

$$ D = B^2 - 4AC < 0 $$

Now,

$$ D=(2\sqrt{2}\,b)^2 - 4ac = 8b^2 - 4ac $$

Thus,

$$ 8b^2 - 4ac < 0 $$

Dividing by $4$:

$$ 2b^2 - ac < 0 $$

So the required condition is

$$ ac > 2b^2 $$

Now $a,b,c \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, so total number of choices is

$$ 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64 $$

We count favourable triples $(a,b,c)$.

Case 1: $b=1$

Then

$$ ac > 2 $$

Now $a,c \in \{1,2,3,4\}$. Total pairs $(a,c)$ are $16$.

We exclude pairs with $ac \le 2$:

  • $(1,1)$ gives $ac=1$

  • $(1,2)$ gives $ac=2$

  • $(2,1)$ gives $ac=2$

So favourable pairs:

$$ 16-3=13 $$

Case 2: $b=2$

Then

$$ ac > 8 $$

Possible products greater than $8$ are obtained from:

  • $(3,3)$ gives $9$

  • $(3,4)$ gives $12$

  • $(4,3)$ gives $12$

  • $(4,4)$ gives $16$

So favourable pairs = $4$.

Case 3: $b=3$

Then

$$ ac > 18 $$

But maximum possible value of $ac$ is

$$ 4 \cdot 4 = 16 $$

So no favourable pair.

Case 4: $b=4$

Then

$$ ac > 32 $$

Again impossible, since maximum $ac=16$.

So no favourable pair.

Hence total favourable triples are

$$ 13+4+0+0 = 17 $$

Therefore, the required probability is

$$ \frac{17}{64} $$

Thus,

$$ m=17,\quad n=64 $$

and

$$ m+n=17+64=81 $$

So the answer is

$$ \boxed{81} $$

questions.questionText: If $\sum\limits_{k=1}^{n} a_k = 6 n^3$, then $\sum\limits_{k=1}^{6} \left( \frac{a_{k+1} - a_k}{36} \right)^2$ is equal to ________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n = 6n^3.$$

To get a formula for one term, write the sum up to $n+1$ terms:

$$a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n + a_{n+1} = 6(n+1)^3$$

Now subtract the first equation from this to isolate $a_{n+1}$:

$$a_{n+1} = 6(n+1)^3 - 6n^3$$

Use the identity $x^3-y^3=(x-y)(x^2+xy+y^2)$ with $x=n+1$ and $y=n$:

$$= 6((n+1)-n)\left((n+1)^2 + n^2 + n(n+1)\right)$$

Since $(n+1)-n=1$, simplify the bracket:

$$a_{n+1} = 6(1)(3n^2 + 3n + 1)$$

So, replacing $n$ by $n-1$ gives a formula for $a_n$:

$$a_n = 6\left(3(n-1)^2 + 3(n-1) + 1\right)$$

Now simplify inside:

$$= 6\left(3(n^2 - 2n + 1 + n - 1) + 1\right)$$

Combine terms:

$$= 6(3(n^2 - n) + 1)$$

So we get:

$$a_n = 6(3n^2 - 3n + 1)$$

Now compute $a_{k+1}-a_k$ using the formulas:

$$\sum\limits_{k=1}^{6} \left(\frac{6(3k^2 + 3k + 1) - 6(3k^2 - 3k + 1)}{36}\right)^2$$

Inside the bracket, cancel common parts:

$$\sum\limits_{k=1}^{6} \left(\frac{36k}{36}\right)^2 = \sum\limits_{k=1}^{6} k^2 = \frac{6 \times 7 \times 13}{6} = 91$$
